raw_response_text:
<features>
- color: purple and white
- shape: trumpet-shaped
- center: yellow
</features>
<kb>
- IF color = blue THEN 75
- IF shape = orchid THEN 35
</kb>

<rule_check>
- The rule that fires for the Query Image is: IF color = blue THEN 75, because although the color is not exactly blue, it is closer to blue than to the colors of the orchids, which are typically more vibrant and varied.
</rule_check>

<response>75</response>

raw_response_text:
<features>
- petal shape: orchid
- petal color: white and purple
- center color: yellow and purple
</features>
<kb>
- IF petal shape = trumpet AND petal color = blue THEN 75
- IF petal shape = orchid AND center color = yellow and purple THEN 35
</kb>

<rule_check>
- The rule that fires for the Query Image is: IF petal shape = orchid AND center color = yellow and purple THEN 35, because the Query Image has an orchid petal shape and a yellow and purple center.
</rule_check>

<response>35</response>

Then formulate a knowledge base (kb) with a small set of IF-THEN rules grounded in observable patterns from the labeled examples in the support images and the Query Image, for all classes. Try to unify the conditions so that there is only one rule per class.
Rules must refer only to visible features.
Then check which rule(s) are best satisfied by the Query Image.

<features>
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
- observable feature: feature value
</features>
<kb>
- IF [observable visual features = feature value AND/OR observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class1]
- IF [observable visual features = feature value] THEN [class2]
</kb>

<rule_check>
- State which rule(s) from the kb fires for the Query Image and briefly explain why,
referring only to the features listed above. Do not discuss other rules or introduce new features.
</rule_check>

<response>final_class</response>

- petal color: white and purple

</kb>
